title: Health Laboratory Practitioners (Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct) Regulations, 2010
title_en: Health Laboratory Practitioners (Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct) Regulations, 2010

provisions.title: Citation
provisions.content: These Regulations may be cited as the Health Laboratory Practitioners (Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct Regulations, 2010 and shall come into operation on the date of publication).

provisions.title: Interpretation
provisions.content: In these Regulations unless the context otherwise requires; “ Act ” means the Health Laboratory Practitioners Act, 2007. Part II – Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct for Health Laboratory Practitioners

provisions.title: Principles
provisions.content: The principles for Ethics and Professional Conduct for Health Laboratory Practitioners shall be: (a) obligation to do good; (b) confidentiality and privacy; (c) accountability; (d) competence and professional advancement; (e) truthfulness fidelity and loyalty; (f) safety and protection; (g) justice; (h) good relationship; and (i) personal presentation and attire.

provisions.title: Obligation to do good
provisions.content: (1) Health Laboratory Practitioners shall be responsible for the overall management of a client’s specimen, investigation, testing and production of timely correct results. (2) The obligation to do good and to avoid doing harm is a human value which guides the Health Laboratory Practitioner to act positively to safeguard and promote the interest of the individual, client and society in general. (3) In so doing, the Health Laboratory Practitioner shall; (a) be constantly on guard in judgement in the face of many outside pressures that may be exerted on him; (b) be entitled to decline to collect specimen and conduct health Laboratory investigations, which believes to be inappropriate or detrimental to the client’s welfare; (c) refuse to condone, facilitate, allow or coordinate laboratory investigation which are detrimental to the client's welfare; (d) practice his profession with consience and dignity; (e) not delegate to a person who is not a qualified Health Laboratory Practitioner or lisenced person task of health laboratory work; and (f) take appropriate action if the workload and pressure, on professional colleagues and juniors may endanger safe standards of practice; (g) uphold and maintain dignity and respect of profession and strike for maintaining a reputation of honesty, integrity and reliability.

provisions.title: Confidentiality and privacy
provisions.content: (1) Confidentiality is the principle, which refers to limitation to access to clients private information; it is a foundation upon which a Health Laboratory Practitioner’s relationship with a client is built and it underlies the trust and willingness of the client that allow the Health Laboratory Practitioner to access the client’s information. (2) Thus the Health Laboratory Practitioner shall— (a) respect the confidential and personal nature of professional records; (b) protect the patient’s right to privacy by keeping the information in the strictest confidentiality; (c) be aware that the information a patient gives to the Health Laboratory Practitioner remains the property of the patient in that respect shall be confidential; and (d) be aware that the findings, which the Health Laboratory Practitioners obtain as a result of laboratory investigations, remains to be confidential. (3) The exceptions to the principles of confidentiality whereby the confidential information can be disclosed shall be where; (a) a patient gives consent to disclosure; (b) the clinician or other authorized health care professional ordering laboratory investigation on patient requests the results; (c) information is required by the due legal processes; or (d) information required, is not be shared with other Health Laboratory Practitioners or other members of health professions given in the official clinical reports.

provisions.title: Accountability
provisions.content: (1) Accountability and responsibility shall be based on the context that the Health Laboratory Practitioner is ready and willing to ensure and account for any action he has taken, the consequence arising and to accept fault from such action. (2) Thus the Health Laboratory Practitioners shall— (a) comply with provisions of the Act and other laws regulating the profession; (b) observe professional responsibilities for practice and adhere to standards of practice and total Health Laboratory Quality Systems; (c) only accept requests for laboratory investigations or analysis which are properly authorized in accordance with established or recognized criteria, and other written laws; (d) be accountable for his work; (e) in the event that an accident is occasioned to a client in the course of management, inform immediately the relevant authority measures taken or to be undertaken; (f) handle the client and any person accompanying with due courtesy and respect; (g) avoid interpersonal relationships that can impair professional judgement and proper assessment of risk; (h) be responsible for the logical process from the acquisition of the specimens to the production of data and the final report of the results; and (i) be accountable for the quality and integrity of health laboratory services;

provisions.title: Competence and professional advancement
provisions.content: Every Health Laboratory Practitioner shall— (a) perform his work within the scope of his competence and professional limitations; (b) take every reasonable opportunity to improve and sustain his knowledge, skills and professional competence; (c) request for support when he perceives the need; (d) provide expert advice and consult other health professionals; (e) be dedicated to the use of health laboratory sciences to benefit mankind; (f) exercise professional judgement, skills and care while meeting established standards and quality systems; (g) strive for improvement of professional skills and knowledge and adopt scientific advancement that benefits the patient and other users; (h) strive for improvement of the delivery of reliable test results, and (i) hold himself out as a person who, by training and experience, is professionally qualified for diagnoses;

provisions.title: Truthfulness, fidelity and loyalty
provisions.content: (1) The principle of truthfulness imposes duty on the Health Laboratory Practitioners to be honest and truthful in the course of their practice. (2) The Health Laboratory Practitioner is obliged to respect the inherent trust that exists in his relationship with the clients. It requires him to communicate truthfully and without deception or deceit. (3) In the event of adverse events to a procedure, device, chemical, or reagent, the Health Laboratory Practitioner shall communicate that information immediately to relevant authorities and keep record in occurance logbook. (4) The Health Laboratory Practitioner shall observe fidelity' by keeping promises that are made and requires not representing in a false or misleading manner. (5) The Health Laboratory Practitioner shall not perform or recommend laboratory investigation procedures that are not necessary or share fees for providing laboratory services in all alse or misleading manner.

provisions.title: Safety and protection
provisions.content: (1) Workers in Health Laboratories are exposed to many dangers, not only from infected material, but also from the dangerous compounds and apparatus which they use in their daily routine. Similary, laboratory and equipment need to be protected from fire and other hazards, to minimize the risk of infections and accidents, certain safety precautions must be enforced. (2) These precautions should be observed by all members of the laboratory staff to protect themselves and those who are obliged to visit the laboratory. (3) In so observing, the Health Laboratory Practitioners shall — (a) follow safe working practices to ensure that patients and others arc not put at risk; (b) know what to do should any accident or fire occur and how to apply emergancy First Aid; (c) use equipment and laboratory reagents correctly and avoid wastage of reagents or other laboratory supplies; (d) properly de-contaminate all bench surfaces, wastes or re-usable materials before disposal or re-use; (e) keep laboratory premises in clean and tidy conditions at all time, and;

provisions.title: Justice
provisions.content: (1) Justice is manifested in fairness, equitableness and impartiality in what is due or required by another person. Health care systems, including health laboratory services, face shortages and scarcity of resources. (2) Given the circumstances, a Health Laboratory Practitioner shall— (a) use wisdom and judgement to ensure that the limited resources are used fairly; and (b) treat all clients fairly and equitably based on needs, regardless of factors such as economic status, religion, race, sex, tribe, age, political affiliation or physical attributes.

provisions.title: Relationship
provisions.content: (1) Trust and honesty are the benchmarks of the relationship between a Health Laboratory Practitioner, clinician, patient and other stakeholders. (2) With reference to relationship with clients and patients, it is the duty of every Health Laboratory Practitioner to: (a) inform a person with whom he has a professional contact, the nature of the relationship; (b) respect the customs, value, spiritual beliefs and human dignity of clients and patients; (c) respect the physical and physiological neds of clients and patients; (d) avoid any abuse of the privilege relationship with clients and patients; (e) promote and safeguard the well being and interests of clients of clients and patients; (f) refuse to accept any gift, favor or hospitality that might be interpreted as seeking to exegete under infuence to obtain preferential treatment; (g) take into consideration that client, patients and accompanying; (h) relatives can easily be embarrased, ashamed or fear in the course of collecting specimen for laboratory investigations. Thus Health Laboratory Practitioner shall:— (i) show humanity, kindness and understanding towards them; (ii) do his best to relieve them of hidden fears and apprehension; (iii) encourage and restore confidence in the client with regard consent to laboratory investigation. (3) With regard to consent to laboratory investigations; The trust of patients and clients that his consent to laboratory investigation will not be misused is essential in the relationship with the Health Laboratory Practitioner, for Health Laboratory Practitioner, even to touch the patient without consent is an assault. Thus- (a) consent shall be valid only when given freely and readily if the client understands the nature, purpose and consequences of what is proposed; and (b) assumed consent or consent obtained by undue influence is valueless. It is thus unethical to carry out health laboratory investigation on a client or patient if there is no direct benefit.

provisions.title: Cooperation with colleagues
provisions.content: Every Health Laboratory Practitioner shall have a responsibility to cooperate professionally with colleagues and other health related professionals, in so doing, a Health Laboratory Practitioner shall; (a) respect each other; (b) share knowledge, skills and experiences with colleagues; (c) avoid any action, which may be regarded as self-praise, condemning or belitlling coleagues or using derogatory language against any of them; and (d) recognize and respect the expertise and contribution of other workers; and collaborate with them to provide the laboratory investigation services and care;

provisions.title: Person presentation and attire
provisions.content: At a place of work, a Health Laboratory Practitioner shall— (a) appear smart, properly and decently dressed in proper attire with identification tags and behave in a manner becoming of professional; (b) be at all times courteous to patients and clients, and be considerate to colleagues; (c) do go consumer alcohol or take unprescribed drugs that could interfere with his performance during laboratory working hours or when on emergency stand-by; and (d) not be involved in any substance abuse. Part III – Special situations

provisions.title: Research and consultancy
provisions.content: Advancement of Health Laboratory Science depends on doing research and consultancy and involve met of experimentation on human subjects, in the circumstances, the Health Laboratory Practitioner shall:— (a) ensure protection of those welfare and rights of subjects used in research; (b) ensure that research is conducted after approval by an approved research Ethical Review Commitee and only qualified persons are allowed to conduct a research;

provisions.title: HIV/AIDS
provisions.content: (1) In the testing and diagnosis of HIV/AIDS, on the Health Laboratory Prctitioner may be faced with ethical problems associated with the natural history of the disease, absence of cure, stigma and accompanying social economical problems. (2) Thus, the Health Laboratory Practitioner shall: (a) observe confidentialy in saving human life against deliberate acts likely to infect potential victims; (b) aquire adequate knowledge of HIV/AIDS transmission, prevention and skills on counseling; (c) educate individuals infected with HIV/AIDS on the opportunity to egree or decline from being research subjects and on the methods applied for scientific research participants and teaching, including taking of photographs; and (d) have a duty to take appropriate precautions to protect their patients and staff from cross-infection.

provisions.title: Disaster and emergency situations
provisions.content: (1) The Health Laboratory Practitioner shall practice his profession with conscience and dignity and the health of the clients shall be his first consideration. (2) Subject to sub regulation (1) , the Health Laboratory Practitioner shall— (a) have an obligation during the threatening emergencies to take immediate steps to ensure that necessary investigations and test are performed to the patients specimens without discrimination or undue delay; (b) educating individuals, where necessary; and (c) acquire adequate knowledge of emerging diseases.

provisions.title: Publicity advertisement and canvassing
provisions.content: (1) Good relationship between a Health Laboratory Practitioner and colleagues is essential for fostering teamwork in the health laboratory profession, thus a Health Laboratory Practitioner shall take cognisance that matters of general interest are sacred and refrain from adopting methods aimed at advertising a particular person, institution, test or technique. (2) Subject to sub paragraph (1), a Health Laboratory Practitioner shall not advertise a sign using professional qualifications to encourage the sale of commercial products: (a) use any professional premises to display the name of commercial product; (b) publish any article or personal photograph or otherwise indulge in any form of self-advertisement or publicity; (c) encourage any practice, which is of a nature that invites attention to professional position, skill, and qualification of achievements; (d) publish or cause to be published in a lay media articles likely to publicize, advertise and canvass; (e) communicate or address to the lay public, use or permit the use of personal professional qualification as an advertisement for the organization or the company or be personally involved in advertising company services; (f) talk in a derogatory manner about the professional skills; know ledge, qualification or services of another practitioner; or (g) canvass any personal for any purposes, whether director indirectly;

provisions.title: Conflict of interest
provisions.content: The Health Laboratory Practitoner shall abide to regulations and avoid situations, which may create conflicts of interest including observation of the Government Procurement Acting in obtaining supplies.

provisions.title: The pledge
provisions.content: (1) A pledge or oath is a promise for commitment to a abide to Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct in accomplishment of professional duties. The pledge shall be taken to mark the fulfillment of profession requirements before the Chancellor of the relevant University, Training Institution or relevant recognized legal authority. (2) The Oath shall be as follows:— I________________________________ solemnly swear/ affirm in the presence of this assembly, that I will fulfill my duties with loyalty and I will endeavor to maintain and elevate the standards of my profession. I will hold in confidence and confidentiality all information availed to me during the practice of the profession. In the course of my practice, I will not accept or give a bribe in exchange for service, perform any tests contrary to established procedures, give false results or do any other action will endanger the health of clients, my colleagues or the safety of the environment. SO HELP ME GOD.
